speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, interview with Eva Najnudel Kimel, January 23, 1996. There's just one more thing about the ghetto. Do you remember what you wore there? What kind of clothes did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that these were the clothes that I still had from back-- back way. I mean, from way back. Because I mean, you didn't buy anything. You didn't-- you couldn't-- you couldn't buy anything. All I remember-- it's interesting. I was playing-- oh, you were asking me about synagogue. OK, now I just remember, yeah. I remember it was Saturday. And Saturday, you know, when the Germans were not around, whatever, I guess people were observing. And I remember there were some-- two boys. And one of them had a new-- a new suit that the parent-- he put on. It was for Saturday. And after-- I don't remember whether there was a synagogue and people went to synagogue. That, I don't remember, but maybe. Because afterwards, we started all playing. And this boy somehow ended up on the fence. You know, he caught-- he was caught on the fence. And he tore that suit. And I remember, that was such a tragedy at that time. That was really-- you couldn't even imagine. Because here, there was this new suit. And it was torn. And there was no one to be gotten again. So that was an incident that all of a sudden just pop up to my-- popped in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, so there was a way of releasing some tension by playing and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think there was for the grownups. The grownups probably were getting together and talking about the situation more and trying to make out-- something out of it and be able to get some food on the table, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmhm. We left you when you were leaving the ghetto. You were escaping from the ghetto. By the way, who guarded the ghetto? Ukrainians or Russians-- or Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainians. How did you get out? How did-- how were you able to get through the gates, or the barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess in-- maybe in some parts of it, maybe it-- maybe there was just wooden fence. And maybe, you know, somebody broke out the-- what do you call these? Pieces of the wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh. Slats.

speaker: interviewee
text: The slats. Yeah, somebody probably broke out the slats. And that's how-- that's how people escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When you left the Polish house that wouldn't take you in, or the Ukrainian house that wouldn't take you in, and you say your mother found a potato shed and there were people in there. Could you describe what the shed-- what the-- it's a cellar kind of thing. How big was it? And how deep was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite-- it was quite deep. It had to be, because people could stand. Because we had-- there was no room to sit down there. And whatever on the outside, it looked really like a dog house.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was like a cone with-- with an opening, with a door, which was like a-- like a circ-- like a semicircle on top. And it had to be quite deep, because otherwise, you know, grownups couldn't stand there. So maybe it was two by four or maybe smaller. Maybe two by two. I don't-- I mean, I see it in front of me, but I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was built by the farmer or the peasant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because this was all-- all around it. It was really like a farm-- farmland, really. There were small villages with houses on quite-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there potatoes there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, you mean in the cellar? No. Because what had happened-- that was-- I think that was the end of the summer, probably. Because otherwise, we could not have walked through the river, because it would have been frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked in the water. I remember that. So it had to be end of the summer. And usually, the potatoes-- they would probably have it only in the fall. So we were lucky that it happened when it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whom did you find in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some other Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. A lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were not a lot. There maybe were five people, six people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know them from before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably yes. I mean, I don't remember knowing them. But probably yes. We probably did. Because the ghetto wasn't that big, really. So I guess everybody knew everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they feel about children being in there, or more people coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, good question, that they were not afraid that somebody's going to scream, or yell, or cry, or whatever. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told to be quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were told to be quiet, absolutely. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then more people joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then another maybe two or three people joined us. That was it. That was it. And we were petrified. When-- you know, when the door opened, we thought this was it. We thought the Germans were there. Because the Germans were walking with dogs, back and forth, and passing back and forth. So although we thought these dogs were so well trained, but I guess they were not that well trained. Because otherwise, they could have probably smelled us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people? I don't mean names, necessarily. But were they younger people, older people? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- my mother was very young at that time. So I'm sure that were-- I don't remember who they were, really. But I think they were all younger people. The older people would not have-- probably did not escape at all. They were all probably gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you went out in the middle of the night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when it got quiet. Who sort of was the leader in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of the people that were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody took responsibility.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because, I guess, they were young and they were not scared. And they-- maybe they knew the area a little bit. Maybe they-- maybe they were swimming in there, in the river. Because that was, you know, before then, before the war. Or maybe they were from the area. And they knew. And they-- they really walked us, or led us, into the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you were, at that point, about 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- that was 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 11 or 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was five years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five and a half years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When you left, can you describe how you went out of there and when you-- how you got to the Bug?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think somebody must have pushed me up, you know in the cellar, and somebody had to pull me up. So I think two people were helping me out. And then we got to the Bug. And as I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all leave at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We all left at the same time. And when we got to the Bug, oh, I remember the water in front of us. And you know, my mother didn't know how to swim. Well, I was a young girl. I had no-- I didn't know how to swim. My sister was a baby. So we were a little-- we were very nervous, because we didn't know how we were going across that Bug. But as I say, it had to be-- it probably was not that deep. So but I think that my mother carried my sister. And me carried some-- some young man, I think, carried me. Because otherwise, I would have never crossed the Bug.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there no Germans on the other side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because the Germans were very scared of forests. So the Germans-- that's how we survived altogether, because they were very nervous. They were scared to get into the forests. They were scared of the partisans. And they were scared that they were going to be killed. So they-- they did not get into those forests.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started-- I don't remember exactly where was the first stop and whatever. But they were certain-- first, I think the people built like a chalet-- I mean, chalet. It wasn't a chalet in a whatever. They took [INAUDIBLE]. And how did they do it? I don't know, whether they found dead-- dead, you know, stamps-- I mean, trees, and they could break them. Because I don't even remember. I mean, I don't know where they got the-- a saw or some tools to build it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they built like a some kind of a chalet? Not a chalet. With an open-- you know, open on the top. And just put, like, the woods, you know, like one next to the other. And we slept on the-- we slept on the ground. And we had a fire in the middle. And the smoke was just going out there. And how many people stayed in each one of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think all of us that-- all of us that-- so maybe it was 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you stayed together with them the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the whole time, but part of the time we stayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job? What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe to go and pick berries, because, you know, there were berries and there were cranberries in the forest. So I was going out and picking berries and picking woods for the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far could you go from there? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually we learned how far we could go and how far we couldn't. We had no watches. We had no idea of time at all. But so what I learned-- I learned to look at the sun. And I knew, well, if the sun was here, then it was midday, or it was in the afternoon, or it was whatever. So that's how-- that's how we were going. Later on, I guess, the older people discovered the villages around-- around that area. And they started venturing out at night to those villages. And they started to steal potatoes from the fields. And we came back and we baked those potatoes. Or they started stealing some other things. When they found out that there were no Germans in the area, then they would start going in and knocking at the peasants' houses and beg for food. And some people were nice and they gave food. And some people said, go away, Jew, because I'm going to call the German on you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go into these villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother used to do it in the beginning. And later on, she wanted me to go. I just couldn't do it. I just couldn't do it in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother went, did she go alone, or did she go with others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she went-- she went alone. She went alone. She was-- as I said, she was a good looking woman, young. So-- and I guess some of the people felt sorry for her, or liked her, or whatever it is. And they gave her food. So sometimes she would come with bread and sometimes with cheese.

speaker: interviewer
text: She'd got at night or in the daytime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when she went to the houses, she went during the day. Because at night, nobody would open the doors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any danger for a young woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any kind of narrow escapes or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --times when you were detected--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or thought you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well, during some of these-- of these times, and exactly I don't remember in which period of time, my mother, by probably going to the village and developing some kind-- some kind of a relation with the people, told them about me. And they had a blind child. And I guess they were both peasants. They both had to work in the fields. And they needed somebody to stay with the blind child and be kind of a nanny to that child. And my mother gave me-- sent me there. They took me in. And I think for three or four months, I was a nanny to this blind child.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived there or you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --went home-- back to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I lived-- I lived there. And that was already, considering living in the forests and whatever it is, this was a big luxury. So I was helping them. And I was-- I was a maid and taking care of this-- of this child. They liked me. They were nice to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them, their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. And I guess they probably-- either some neighbors discovered that they were having a Jewish girl, because that was happening quite often, or they heard maybe the Germans were coming, or whatever it is. So they send me-- they send me back. Now, my sister, on the other hand-- my mother again found some-- found some Poles or Ukrainians, too, and again developed a relationship with them. And they took my sister in. And they said, OK, they are going to tell the neighbors that she's a cousin who came-- whose parents passed away, or whatever. And she came to live with them from whatever. My sister was dark. And she was with them for maybe a couple of weeks or a month. And she developed sickness that you get like-- it's contagious. And I don't know how it's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ringworm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very contagious and it's very-- it's like pimples all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know-- and it was infectious disease, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when they disc-- when they found that-- when they found that she was sick, they somehow got in touch with my mother. And they told my mother to take her. So my mother went and picked her up. And she was walking through the village. And there was a German. And all of the southern, a German comes over to her and says, [GERMAN]? And my sister, at that moment, says, 'Mama.' When they heard that she spoke Polish, they let them go. So that was one of very close calls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to see your mother while you were working as a nanny or as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe my mother did come and visit. I don't remember. I really don't remember. Maybe I got some food from them and I passed on to my mother. It is possible, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep there? With this child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, children didn't have their own rooms there. There was one big room. There was a big oven, like a baker's oven, where you bake bread-- this type of an oven-- clay ovens, they were. And near the ovens, there was a place that you could sleep. It was really part of the oven. And that was a warm part of the oven. So everybody tried to sleep on that oven, really, because that was the warmest part. But I didn't. I think I slept in-- you know, in one of the rooms, on a piece of-- you know, some kind of a-- like a bed made out of wood or, you know, whatever-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have other children, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that they had that only child. Then, in another part, my mother gave me to-- to other peasants. And I was working as a cowgirl. And I had to take out the cows to-- you know, to the pasture and spend the whole day with the cows. And I had to take them out in the morning and then bring them back at night. And they were treating me nice. At least I had butter, and I had milk, and I had foot to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed with them as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed with them as well, right. And I remember that I used to sleepwalk at night. And very often, I would find myself outside the house, just-- and then they would, I guess, hear whatever. They would bring me back in. So during the day-- I mean, while I was on the pasture, there was their son, too, or somebody else. So you know, we were playing. And we were in the-- picking whatever there was to pick. And that was another part of that-- of that ordeal. My sister was mostly with my mother. Then, we were-- after-- after living in this-- in those huts or whatever-- hut, really, not chalet-- we would go to-- we would, again, have to move. Because somebody would say that Germans are close by. So we would move. And my mother would, again, develop some relation with some Poles. And they would let us stay on the-- over the-- over a barn, in-- you know, in straw upstairs. So we would be laying there the whole day, not be able to move. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you and your mother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the other people, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just me and my mother and my sister. And I don't remember if my uncle was-- whether he was with us or not at that time. So we were hiding there. And at night, they would bring us some food. And when we had to go to the bathroom, it was going at night. We had to hold the whole day. We couldn't-- couldn't walk down from there. And again, we heard people coming, and neighbors, and whatever. And everybody was snooping because whatever. Then, again, when we were traveling from one village to another, from one place of the forest to another, we would end up in somebody's field and sleep in a hay-- in a big hay--

speaker: interviewer
text: Haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: --haystack. You know, my mother would go in and just make-- like, pull out something. And the hay is very warm. So we could survive. And in the middle of the night, we would hear dogs barking. And we would hear somebody would say, oh my god. What's happening now? Why that the dog is barking now? Well, maybe the Germans are around the corner-- and you know, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the structure that they put up for what were you-- you say about a dozen people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --staying there with-- wasn't the smoke, where you had your fire in the middle-- wasn't that detected? Wouldn't it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but it would have been, again. But these forests were quite thick. And the forests were quite big. And we were not exactly next to a village. We were really in the middle of a forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other-- near you, other people like you in other little--

speaker: interviewee
text: There probably were. There probably were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there contact? But

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with any partisan groups at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, no. Maybe later on, maybe. But not-- not at that-- that was really the initial time. Because there was a time when we ended up in [NON-ENGLISH], there, where we were already, or not far from there. And again, we somehow-- we were in the forest. And my mother, again, went to town. And some Poles gave her a little room. My uncle was with us at that time. And the Pole that gave us this little apartment, or this little room-- whatever-- maybe my mother did have some money at that time. Maybe she did have. Yes, she did have. Because I remember, she used to wear like a corset. And in that corset, in the pocket, she did have some money. So in some instances--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the money come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess-- I guess she must have taken it from [NON-ENGLISH]. Maybe she always wore that corset, because she was afraid probably. She was afraid to leave it. There were so many people, that somebody will steal it. She-- I think she always wore it, now when I think about it. So it was like part of her, really. When she got up, when she got dressed in the morning, that's what she wore. And the money was sewed in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember this incident. She was-- so he gave us this little apartment. And his parents lived in the same house, or the same-- next house. He was married. And he wanted my mother. You know, he said, I'll leave you here. I won't tell them anything. But you have to have sex with me. And my mother was avoiding it by giving all kinds of excuses, really. She can't. She's not-- da, da, da, da, da, until it got to a point where he finally kicked us out from there. Because he couldn't get it, he kicked us out. And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- before we end this, I don't remember whether that was in this particular place, where I developed rheumatic fever. My uncle got typhoid. And then, later on, my mother got typhoid. I mean, thank God both of them survived. The typhoid, I guess, was not that severe. Now, I with that-- with my rheumatic fever, I was sick for about half a year. I walked out, outside, because there was-- as I said, there was no plumbing. There was nothing. I went outside. And I had to release myself, or I fell. I couldn't get up. That was it. For half a year, I couldn't walk. I couldn't walk. And I couldn't-- I was incapacitated. I couldn't do anything with my hands and I couldn't walk. So I stayed in bed for about half a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I think we were in this guy's house. But what had happened, that at that time, my mother had developed somehow-- there was a priest, with whom my parents-- my mother, not my-- my father was not alive anymore. My mother developed, you know, some kind of a relationship because she started begging, I think. When she was begging, she went. She knocked at this priest's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the house-- what do they call it? The house-- Housekeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the housekeeper was very nice. And she was giving my mother food. And then the priest found out about us, or learned about us. And he was a human being. And he started helping us. And being, I guess, that he developed good relation with us, the people in the village somehow tolerated us. I don't know if everybody knew, or who knew, or who whatever. But he was helping us. And he sent us a Polish doctor who was in that village. And this doctor was coming, and visiting with me, and was helping my mother during the typhoid, and my uncle during the typhoid, and me with-- with this rheumatic fever. He was giving me some kind of a medication. I don't know what it was. And after half a year, I started to learn how to hold a cup in my hand again. It was like completely new experience. And I remember, when I made my first step after that, I was like a baby who was-- you know, who is 18 months old and starts to walk. Somebody had to stand in front of me. And I was making steps and running to that person. The person would be catching me. And that's how finally I got back on my feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living then? In that-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember which house we were in, but somewhere, in somebody's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you always have contact with the original group that crossed the Bug?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. Maybe we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to the woods with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what had happened is that when we were in these villages, every so often, you know, the Poles would come to us, that we were staying with or that were helping us-- whatever. And they would say, I think the Germans-- the Germans are coming. So when that was, we had to run to the forest. And at that time, some other group of Jews-- or maybe that was the one that we were, you know, in contact with. I don't really remember. They had built already a winter structure. Because sometimes we had to run away. And that was one time that I had with my legs that I couldn't walk, that we had to run away there, because they were afraid that if the Germans come to the village, everybody's going to be killed. So again, this, the winter structure, was built in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: How deep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it had to be-- you know, it had to be for a person to practically stand up. Maybe a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it was maybe for 20 people. And again, there was an opening on the top for the fire, because that was the only way for us to keep warm. And you slept-- they built from the-- from a trees-- you know, like from 10 trees, they built like kind of a benches or whatever that you slept on. And a few times, we had to-- we ended up there-- to sit through that scary part of time, when they were telling us the Germans are coming into the villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they find out? How did they know about it? I don't know. But that was happening. Were you with the original group all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did one get accepted into one of these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Groups?

speaker: interviewer
text: Groups. Were there other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the villages. I don't remember any children in those-- in the house that we stayed. But in the villages, where we stayed, I guess there were some other families that were hidden. And I remember particular about a girl who I was friendly with-- again, I don't remember anybody's name. It's terrible. And she was hidden under a down quilt. But the quilts are not like quilts that we know them today. There was like a big, you know, thick-- maybe this-- this thick. And this child was there the whole day, under that quilt, with the bed all made up, without-- I don't know how she-- how she was getting air.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I meant in the actual woods, in the huts and the underground thing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't remember. What utensils did they use? How could they build? Did they have shovels, and picks, and stuff like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe-- you know, they probably were stealing them from the-- you know, from the peasants. Because I mean, nothing was really locked. I mean, and these peasants lived on pieces-- big pieces of, you know, properties. And everything was done with a shovel and very primitive-- you know, with the saw, and the shovel, and whatever. They were no combines. There were no tractors. There were nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would assume that they were going into these barns, into these-- you know, to the fields, like they were digging potatoes with-- you know, with shovels. And probably that's-- that's what happened. Because I mean, that was the only way to survive. Do you remember the name of the villages, or the village you were in, or any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember one was, you know-- one was this [NON-ENGLISH] that we were hidden. One was [NON-ENGLISH], where we moved after already, after the war. I don't remember the others, no. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change our tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boy, I didn't think that that's--
